title: Understanding Literals and Variables

descriptions.1:
Now that we know about variables, we can contrast them with literals to build a deeper understanding of how coding works.
Having this vocabulary in your toolbox will help you understand how to write code more effectively.
A literal is simply a value written directly in the code.
descriptions.2:
Here are some examples of literals:
- Numbers: 42, 3.14, -7
- Text (strings): "Hello", "Python"
- True/False (boolean): True, False
descriptions.3: Think of literals as 'hardcoded' values. We can use them directly or store them in variables.
descriptions.4: Let's build a simple program that describes a user. First, we'll use literals to 'hardcode' the information.
descriptions.5:
Hardcoding information makes our program rigid. If we want to change the information, we have to change the code itself.
Let's make it flexible by using variables instead.
descriptions.6: Start by creating a variable for the user's name.
descriptions.7: Now let's make a variable for favorite food.
descriptions.8:
Perfect! Now our message uses variables instead of literals. This makes our program more flexible - we can change the values without changing the code that uses them.
In this code snippet, the "Method 2" approach will print the exact same message as the "Method 1" approach, but it will use variables instead of hardcoded literal values.
For this little program it doesn't make much difference, but we will see later how this can be a powerful feature of programming.
descriptions.9:
Let's review:
1) Literals are values written directly in code (like "Hello" or 42)
2) Variables are containers that can hold these values
3) Using variables makes our code more flexible than hardcoding literals

instructions.4: Supply the text needed to print the message: "My name is Alf. My favorite food is pizza."
instructions.6:
Create a variable named 'name' and assign it the value "Alf".
There is one blank for each part of the assignment (variable name, operator, value).
Don't forget to put double quotes around Alf's name (the literal value).
instructions.7:
Create a variable named 'food' and assign it the value "pizza".
Don't forget the double quotes.
instructions.8:
Plug the variable names for name and food into the message.
Remember: we want to plug in the variable names this time, not the literal values.

template: 3:3?# Printing value with a literal directly
3:3?# Printing value with a variable (same output)
4?print("My name is @@Alf@@. My favorite food is @@pizza@@.")
5?# Method 2: Print a dynamic message using variables.
8?# Create a temporary variable for the message, then print
8?message = "My name is " + @@name@@ + ". My favorite food is " + @@food@@ + "."
